title: Understanding the Problem
text: To find out how much the wire stretches, we need to use the formula for linear deformation in materials, which involves Young's modulus.

title: Formula for Linear Deformation
text: The formula to calculate the stretch (ΔL) of a wire is given by: $$ΔL=\frac{F L}{A Y}$$, where F is the force applied, L is the original length of the wire, A is the cross-sectional area, and Y is Young's modulus.

title: Calculate the Cross-Sectional Area
text: The cross-sectional area (A) of the wire can be calculated using the formula for the area of a circle: $$A=\pi \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2$$, where $$d$$ is the diameter of the wire.

title: Calculate the Cross-Sectional Area
text: Given the diameter of the wire is $$0.50$$ cm, calculate the cross-sectional area in square meters.

title: Substitute Values into the Formula
text: Now that you have the cross-sectional area, substitute the values into the formula $$ΔL=\frac{F L}{A Y}$$ to find the stretch of the wire.

title: Calculate the Stretch
text: Using $$F=3.94\times{10}^3$$ N, $$L=15.0$$ $$m$$, $$A=1.9635\times{10}^{-5}$$ m², and $$Y=2\times{10}^{11}$$ N/m², calculate the stretch of the wire.
